column1:
Here is the plain text explanation of the four strategic clusters. This breakdown explains why these specific sectors are grouped together and how they function as a coherent unit for University of Szczecin:

Cluster 1: Living Resources & Biotechnology (The "Bio-Economy")
The Focus: This cluster is entirely centered on biological life—from the microscopic genetic material of algae to the management of massive fish stocks.

The Logic:
From Extraction to Stewardship: Historically, fisheries were about "hunting." The new logic, driven by the Ocean Pact, is about stewardship (caring for the stock) and cultivation (aquaculture).
High-Tech Value: We group Fisheries with Biotech and R&I because the future value of the ocean isn't just in selling fish fillets; it’s in discovering new pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and biofuels from marine organisms.
Sociological Angle: This is where the concept of the "Ocean Steward" is born. The key skill here isn't just catching fish, but understanding the ecosystem so that the community can survive for generations (intergenerational justice).

Cluster 2: Energy, Resources & Desalination (The "Hard" Economy)
The Focus: This cluster deals with industrial production and engineering within the marine space. It covers offshore wind farms, seabed mining, and freshwater production.

The Logic:
Space is the Resource: Unlike fishing (where fish move), these industries need fixed space. The central challenge here is Maritime Spatial Planning (MSP)—figuring out how to put a wind farm next to a shipping lane without causing conflict.
Decarbonization: This cluster is the "engine room" of the Green Deal. The logic is purely technical and industrial: how to generate maximum power with minimum environmental harm.
Sociological Angle: This is the zone of highest conflict (e.g., fishermen protesting wind farms). The sociological competence needed here is conflict mediation and obtaining a "Social License to Operate."

Cluster 3: Transport, Ports & Infrastructure (The "Blue Spine")
The Focus: This cluster focuses on logistics, movement, and trade. It connects the ocean to the land (Hinterland) via ports, ships, and repair yards.

The Logic:
Efficiency & Data: The global economy relies on this spine. The modern logic is Digitization—using "Digital Twins" to track every container and emission molecule to optimize efficiency.
The Green Transition: Shipping is dirty; the goal here is to retrofit the entire fleet for green fuels (ammonia/hydrogen) and circularity (recycling old ships).
Sociological Angle: This cluster addresses the "Port-City" relationship. The goal is Re-maritimisation—ensuring the port city (like Szczecin) doesn't turn its back on the port, but integrates it culturally and economically.

Cluster 4: Tourism, Defence & Robotics (The "Service & Security" Layer)
The Focus: This cluster encompasses the immaterial (tourism/experience) and the protective (defence/surveillance), enabled by advanced robotics.

The Logic:
Access without Extraction: Tourism sells the experience of the sea; Defence ensures the safety of the sea. Neither extracts physical resources, but both require deep knowledge of the environment.
The Eyes of the Ocean: We include Robotics here because drones and sensors are the "eyes" that allow tourists to see reefs (VR/AR) and navies to see threats (surveillance).
Sociological Angle: This is about Ocean Literacy and Security. It transforms the public from passive observers into active "Ocean Citizens" who understand and value the marine environment.
